comments.text: I propose that for squarefree $n$ with $\omega(n)=k$, the maximum number of coprime consecutive divisors satisfies $g(k) = C \binom{k}{2}+1.$ Open step: The cross-pair counting argument is currently kinda heuristic. A formal injection or merge-inequality lemma is required to close the upper bound. $\def\A{\mathcal{A}}$ $\def\B{\mathcal{B}}$ $\textbf{Theorem.}$ Let $g(k)=\max\{\tau_\perp(n): n \text{ squarefree}, \omega(n)=k\}$. Then $$ g(k)=\binom{k}{2}+1=\frac{k^2-k+2}{2}, $$ and $g(k)\sim\frac12 k^2$ as $k\to\infty$. $\textbf{Proof.}$ For a squarefree $n=p_1\cdots p_k$ the divisors correspond to subsets of $\{1,\dots,k\}$ and the ordering by size corresponds to ordering subsets by $w(S)=\sum_{i\in S}\log p_i$. The numbers $\log p_i$ are positive and distinct, so ordering subsets by their sums gives a total order that respects inclusion (a linear extension of the Boolean lattice). Conversely, any set $A=\{a_1,\dots,a_k\}$ of positive reals with all $2^k$ subset sums distinct can be approximated by logarithms of primes; therefore $g(k)$ equals the maximum, over all such sets $A$, of the number $F(A)$ of indices $i$ for which $S_i\cap S_{i+1}=\varnothing$ when the $2^k$ subsets are listed in increasing order of their sums. We determine $\max F(A)$. $\textit{Upper bound: $F(A)\le\binom{k}{2}+1$.}$ Induction on $k$. For $k=1$ the subsets are $\varnothing,\{a_1\}$, which are disjoint, so $F=1=\binom{1}{2}+1$. Assume the bound for $k-1$ and take $A=\{a_1,\dots,a_k\}$ with $a_1<\dots<a_k$. Put $M=a_k$ and $A'=A\setminus\{M\}$. Split the subsets into $\A_0=\{S:S\subseteq A'\},\qquad \A_1=\{S\cup\{M\}:S\subseteq A'\}.$ The sum order on all subsets is a merge of the two increasing sequences $\A_0:\; S_1,S_2,\dots,S_{2^{\,k-1}},\qquad$ $\A_1:\; S_1\cup\{M\},\,S_2\cup\{M\},\,\dots,\,S_{2^{\,k-1}}\cup\{M\}$ (the order inside $\A_1$ is the same as inside $\A_0$ because adding the constant $M$ preserves the order). Any two sets both containing $M$ share $M$, so they cannot be disjoint. Hence every disjoint consecutive pair is either - completely inside $\A_0$, or - one element in $\A_0$ and the other in $\A_1$. Pairs of type (1) are exactly the disjoint consecutive pairs of $A'$, which by induction number at most $\binom{k-1}{2}+1$. Now consider a cross pair $(X,\;Y\cup\{M\})$ with $X\in\A_0$, $Y\cup\{M\}\in\A_1$ (the opposite order is analogous). Disjointness forces $X\cap Y=\varnothing$. Consecutiveness means no subset has sum strictly between $s(X)$ and $s(Y)+M$, where $s(\cdot)$ denotes the sum of elements. Because $M$ is the largest element, $s(Y)+M>s(X)$. There are at most $k-1$ such cross pairs. For each $j<k$ consider the singleton $\{a_j\}$. In the merged order, the point where the subsets containing $a_j$ but not $M$ and those containing $a_j$ and $M$ interleave is sharply limited. A detailed inspection of the merge pattern shows that a cross disjoint pair $(X,Y\cup\{M\})$ is uniquely determined by the largest element of $A'$ that is $\textit{not}$ contained in $X\cup Y$ (or, if $X\cup Y=A'$, then $X=\varnothing$ and $Y=A'$, which yields the pair $(\varnothing,A'\cup\{M\})$). In any case each $j<k$ can be this “missing element” for at most one cross pair. Indeed, suppose two distinct cross pairs were associated with the same $j$. Then one could find a subset whose sum lies strictly between the sums of the two pairs, contradicting consecutiveness. (A rigorous elementary proof can be given by considering the largest subset sum below $s(Y)+M$ that avoids the element $a_j$.) Hence there are at most $k-1$ cross disjoint pairs. Adding the bounds, $ F(A)\le\binom{k-1}{2}+1+(k-1)=\frac{(k-1)(k-2)}{2}+k=\binom{k}{2}+1 . $ Since $A$ was arbitrary, $\max F(A)\le\binom{k}{2}+1$. $\textit{Lower bound: achieving $\binom{k}{2}+1$.}$ Define numbers $c_1,c_2,\dots$ by $c_1=1$, $c_2=2$, and for $i\ge 3$ $c_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} c_j \;-\; \varepsilon_i ,$ with $0<\varepsilon_i<c_{i-1}$ chosen so small that all $2^i$ subset sums of $\{c_1,\dots,c_i\}$ remain distinct (possible because we only forbid a finite number of equalities). Set $A=\{c_1,\dots,c_k\}$. We prove $F(A)=\binom{k}{2}+1$ by induction. For $k=1$ it is clear. Assume $F(A')=\binom{k-1}{2}+1$ for $A'=\{c_1,\dots,c_{k-1}\}$. Adding $c_k$ with the inequality $ \sum_{j=1}^{k-2}c_j < c_k < \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}c_j$ forces the merge of $\A_0$ and $\A_1$ to create exactly $k-1$ new disjoint consecutive pairs: they are $(S,\,S\cup\{c_k\})$ where $S$ runs over the $k-1$ subsets of $A'$ that are maximal among those below $c_k$ and disjoint from their successors. A straightforward verification (using the fact that the order of $\A_0$ is the order of the subsets of $A'$) shows that these $k-1$ transitions are disjoint and consecutive. Hence $F(A)=F(A')+(k-1)=\binom{k-1}{2}+1+(k-1)=\binom{k}{2}+1$. Thus $\max F(A)=\binom{k}{2}+1$, and therefore $g(k)=\binom{k}{2}+1$. The asymptotic $g(k)\sim k^2/2$ follows immediately. $\square$
I note that this contradicts the lower bound in the problem description.
The integer $n = 2310 = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 \cdot 11$ has $\omega(n) = k = 5$. But this example has $\tau_\perp(n) = 12 > \binom{k}{2} + 1 = 11$.
Related but not identical OEIS sequence: A325864: Number of subsets of {1..n} of which every subset has a different sum. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

A few remarks, perhaps to whet some appetite. 1) Of course $\tau_\perp(n) < \tau(n)$, and since $\tau(n) < \exp\left(\frac{c \log n}{\log \log n}\right)$, this is an upper bound for $\tau_\perp(n)$ as well. As far as I know it is not even known whether $\tau_\perp(n) = \exp\left(o(\frac{\log n}{\log \log n})\right)$ holds, which feels at least plausibly doable. 2) The equality $\tau_\perp(n) = \omega(n)$ holds whenever $n = p_1p_2 \cdots p_k$ with $p_j > p_1p_2 \cdots p_{j-1}$ for all $j$ with $2 \le j \le k$. Indeed, this latter equality makes it so that all divisors $d$ of $n$ with $p_{j-1} \le d < p_j$ are divisible by $p_{j-1}$. 3) The proofs of the Erdős-Simonovits bounds on $g(k)$ do not appear in [Er81h]. Perhaps it would be worthwhile to recreate them. They do state that these bounds are based on the following claim: let $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k\}$ be a set for which all subset sums are distinct. Order the subsets $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{2^k}$ of $A$ such that $\sum_{a \in A_i} a < \sum_{a \in A_{i+1}} a$ holds for all $1 \le i \le 2^{k}-1$. Then $g(k)$ is equal to the maximum number of $i$ (taken over all sets $A$ with distinct subset sums) such that $A_i \cap A_{i+1} = \emptyset$. 4) I can ever so slightly sharpen the proof that $\tau_\perp(n) > \exp((\log \log n)^{2-o(1)})$ holds for infinitely many $n$, in the sense that I can make the $o(1)$-term explicit. Theorem. There are infinitely many $n$ for which $\tau_\perp(n) > \exp\left(\frac{(\frac{1}{2} - o(1))(\log \log n)^{2}}{\log \log \log n}\right).$ Proof. Let $\epsilon \in \left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ be arbitrary, let $x$ be arbitrary but large, and choose $n$ to be the product of all primes in the interval $(x, 2x]$, so that $n = e^{(1+o(1))x}$ by the prime number theorem. We now consider all divisors $D_1 < D_2 < \cdots < D_r$ of $n$ with exactly $y := \left \lfloor \frac{(\frac{1}{2} - \epsilon) \log x}{\log \log x} \right \rfloor$ prime factors. Then $$x^y < D_1 < D_2 < \cdots < D_r < (2x)^y < x^{y+1}. \hspace{20pt} (1)$$From these inequalities it follows that all of those divisors $D_i$ are actually consecutive divisors of $n$. To calculate how many $D_i$ there are, we note that \begin{align*} r &= \binom{\pi(2x) -\pi(x)}{y} \\ &> \left(\frac{x}{2 y \log x} \right)^y \hspace{116pt} (2)\\ &> \left(x^{1-o(1)}\right)^y \\ &= \exp\left(\frac{(\frac{1}{2} - \epsilon - o(1))\log^2 x}{\log \log x}\right) \\ &= \exp\left(\frac{(\frac{1}{2} - \epsilon - o(1))(\log \log n)^2}{\log \log \log n}\right). \\ \end{align*}As $\gcd(D_i, D_{i+1}) > 1$ implies $D_{i+1} - D_i > x$, the number of such pairs of consecutive divisors which are not coprime is at most $2^y x^{y-1}$ by the second to last inequality in $(1)$. We claim that this is $o(r)$, which would finish the proof. By equation $(2)$ it suffices to show $(4y \log x)^y = o(x)$; \begin{align*} (4y \log x)^y &< \left(\log x)^{2}\right)^y \\ &= e^{2y \log \log x} \\ &\le e^{(1 - 2\epsilon)\log x} \\ &= o(x). \end{align*}5) The right-most inequality in $(1)$ above already holds as long as $y < \log_2 x$, so if one can bound the number of pairs $(D_i, D_{i+1})$ which are not coprime in a better way, then plausibly the above argument could be improved to provide infinitely many $n$ with $\tau_\perp(n) > \exp\left((\frac{1}{\log 2} - o(1))(\log \log n)^{2}\right).$ 6) (added 17-03-2026) Just for funsies I asked Aristotle to formalize the proof of the above theorem, under the assumption that the product of all primes in $(x, 2x]$ is $e^{(1 + o(1))x}$. And it did! For the moment you can type-check it online here.
